reasoning: The 4-week Treasury bill rate closely tracks the Effective Federal Funds Rate (EFFR). As of Oct 24, the 4-week rate is at 3.94%, which is already pricing in a highly anticipated 25 basis point rate cut at the October 28-29 FOMC meeting. After this expected cut (bringing the target range down to 3.75%–4.00%), the T-bill rate should settle around 3.75%–3.85% by early November, well below the 3.94% threshold. Because the December 10 FOMC meeting is also heavily expected to result in another rate cut towards the 3.50%–3.75% range, the downward pressure will continue through November and into early 2026. For the rate to increase above the Oct 24 value of 3.94%, the Fed would need to unexpectedly hold rates steady or hike, which is given a low probability (~10%) based on current market futures. In accordance with forecasting principles, uncertainty increases with time, so the likelihood of an unforeseen event (such as an inflation spike causing a pause in rate cuts) pushes the probabilities gradually closer to 0.5 for the later resolution dates.
